n. pl. An order of Entomostraca; -- so named from the
   feet of branchiopods having been supposed to perform the function of
   gills. It includes the fresh-water genera Branchipus, Apus, and
   Limnadia, and the genus Artemia found in salt lakes. It is also called
   Phyllopoda. See Phyllopoda, Cladocera. It is sometimes used in a
   broader sense.

brazil wood.d:
The wood of the oriental Caesalpinia Sapan; -- so called
   before the discovery of America.
 A very heavy wood of a reddish color, imported from
   Brazil and other tropical countries, for cabinet-work, and for dyeing.
   The best is the heartwood of Caesalpinia echinata, a leguminous tree;
   but other trees also yield it. An inferior sort comes from Jamaica, the
   timber of C. Braziliensis and C. crista. This is often distinguished as
   Braziletto , but the better kind is also frequently so named.
